caseInventory: The suite is 25 scenarios in lib/benchmark/gameiq/battleship.ts: 2 hand-authored base scenarios (corner-carrier-hit, follow-line) plus 23 generated from GENERATED_BATTLESHIP_TARGETS (battleship.ts:72-102), all mapped over the SAME untouched default board (createInitialBattleshipState, battleship.ts:114) with one generic prompt string. They ship as the single pack "gameiq-v0.1-battleship" (certificationTier "lightweight", lib/benchmark/gameiq/packs.ts:30-36), which is reachable at runtime: listGameIqScenarioPacks → runCertifiedGameIq (lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts:85-115) → CertifiedRunPanel/suite-options in the UI. Every scenario produces one verifier assertion and feeds outcomeScore/moveQuality/legalActionRate/structuredReliability into scoreGameIqAttempt (lib/benchmark/scoring/gameiq.ts) and the certified leaderboard; no dead cases. However, only 1 of 25 scenarios (follow-line) has any shot history, and the 25 scenarios collapse to roughly 2 distinct decision archetypes: (a) output a ship cell that is both named in the title and visible in the leaked state JSON (18 scenarios), and (b) output a specific empty-water cell named only in the title (7 scenarios).

findings.summary: The full BattleshipGameState — including every cell of the opponent's five ships and shipId labels on shot records — is serialized verbatim into the model-facing prompt, destroying the hidden-information premise of every targeting scenario.
findings.evidence: certified-runner.ts:260 `Initial state JSON: ${JSON.stringify(scenario.initialState)}`; battleship.ts:114 sets initialState = createInitialBattleshipState(), whose boards.orange.ships lists all cells (engine.ts:85-97, e.g. carrier cells {row:0,column:0..4}); shot records carry shipId (engine.ts:460-465). So 'hit the known carrier corner' and every 'target the X' scenario reduce to reading coordinates out of the prompt JSON. Prior review finding #67 even asserted 'the only signal the model is given is that A1 was a hit' — which is factually wrong; the leak was never catalogued.
findings.proposedImprovement: Introduce a model-facing projection (e.g. BattleshipTrackingView { boardSize: 10, yourShots: [{label, result: 'hit'|'miss'|'sunk', sunkShipId?}], remainingShipSizes: number[] }) and pass that as the scenario's prompt state instead of the raw engine state; keep the full state only for verifier-side legality checks (validateBattleshipAction can keep using it). This one change is prerequisite to any of the scenarios measuring targeting rather than JSON reading.
verifierCorrections: Finding is understated in two ways. (a) The leak includes BOTH players' fleets (boards.blue and boards.orange are both serialized), not just "the opponent's five ships". (b) There is a second, independent answer leak the proposed fix must also cover: certified-runner.ts:258 puts scenario.title into the prompt, and for all 23 generated scenarios the title literally names the answer coordinate (e.g. "Battleship: target the battleship top at B3", "choose unrepeated parity corner J10"). A model-facing state projection alone would leave those 23 scenarios trivially answerable from the title; titles/prompts must be scrubbed too. Also note the base "corner-carrier-hit" scenario's initialState (battleship.ts:18 firstShotState) has NO shots fired at all — the "known carrier" is knowable only via the leaked ships or memorization, strengthening the finding.

1) The shipId-on-shot-records leak applies to only 1 of the 25 scenarios (follow-line, via followCarrierLineState with carrier-tagged shots at A1/A2); despite its name, firstShotState is a pristine createInitialBattleshipState() with empty shotsReceived, so 24 of 25 scenarios leak solely via boards.orange.ships (and boards.blue.ships). 2) The leak is broader than stated: gameIqScenarioPrompt also injects scenario.title (certified-runner.ts:258), and all 23 generated titles name the expected coordinate verbatim (e.g. 'target the battleship top at B3', battleship.ts:79-101). The proposed BattleshipTrackingView projection is therefore necessary but not sufficient — generated-scenario titles/prompts must also be rewritten or the answer still ships in the prompt. 3) initialState assignments are at battleship.ts:43, 61, and 114, not just 114.

Two refinements. (1) The proposed BattleshipTrackingView projection alone is insufficient: gameIqScenarioPrompt also injects scenario.title (certified-runner.ts:258), and 23/25 titles literally name the answer coordinate ('target the battleship top at B3', 'choose unrepeated parity corner J10' — battleship.ts:79-101), so scenarios would remain answerable by title-parsing. The fix must also re-author titles/prompts and the single-exact-coordinate expectedActions (under real hidden information, parity/information-gain scenarios have no unique correct answer); note the base 'known carrier corner' scenario has an empty moveHistory and is unanswerable without the leak, so re-authoring is unavoidable. (2) Severity: 'high' is defensible for the pack's validity, but impact is bounded — the pack is certificationTier 'lightweight' (packs.ts:34) and prior finding #67 already established it measures memorization of the fixed default board rather than targeting skill; the novel contribution here is the leak mechanism and the correction of #67's false 'only signal' premise. Also, any state-shape change alters stableGameIqScenarioPackDigest (packs.ts:121-125), so the fix entails a pack version/digest bump as part of the scoring contract.

findings.summary: The scenario title — included verbatim in the prompt (certified-runner.ts:258) — names the exact target cell for all 23 generated scenarios ('follow the carrier line at B1', 'choose unrepeated parity corner J10'), so passing requires only parsing a cell label, not targeting skill.
findings.evidence: battleship.ts:79-101 titles embed the answer coordinate; battleship.ts:112-113 the generic prompt says 'best advances the stated targeting objective' where the objective exists ONLY in the title; certified-runner.ts:257-258 puts `Scenario N of M: ${scenario.title}` in the user prompt. The 2026-07-01 review observed this mechanism only inside a dismissal (review line ~1621, defending exact-match scoring) and never flagged it as leakage.
findings.proposedImprovement: Remove target coordinates from titles entirely; state the objective behaviorally in the prompt ('two collinear hits recorded at A1,A2 — continue the line', 'no shots fired yet — choose a maximum-information first shot') and derive the accepted answer set from the engine. Any scenario whose objective cannot be stated without naming the cell has no real decision and should be deleted.
verifierCorrections: Two precision notes. (a) The title labels use inconsistent coordinate conventions: most are column-letter+row-number ("B1" → {row:0,column:1}, "E6" → {row:5,column:4}), but parity-edge "A10" → {row:0,column:9} and edge-scan "J1" → {row:9,column:0} are row-letter+column-number, and the auto-generated expectedActions label at battleship.ts:117 uniformly uses row-letter (so the "B1" scenario's own expected label is "A2"). Passing therefore requires parsing the label PLUS guessing the per-scenario convention — the leak is real but slightly noisier than "only parsing a cell label" implies, and two scenarios would fail a model that parses consistently. (b) An additional aggravating leak the finding omits: certified-runner.ts:260 serializes the FULL initial state — including all orange ship placements from createInitialBattleshipState — into the prompt, so the board the model is supposedly reasoning about is fully visible, letting it disambiguate ship-anchored title labels and further nullifying any targeting-skill measurement. The review-line reference is ~1616 rather than 1621, immaterial. Severity high is appropriate: all 23 of 25 battleship scenarios are affected.

Three refinements. (a) Coordinate-convention wrinkle: the titles use column-letter/row-number ("B1" = {row:0,column:1}), but the engine's own formatter (lib/games/battleship/engine.ts:26,130 ROW_LABELS) and the generated expectedActions label (battleship.ts:117, which computes "A2" for the "B1" scenario) use row-letter/column-number. So the leak is not pure copy-through — a model parsing "B1" with the engine's (classic Battleship) convention answers {row:1,column:0} and is scored wrong. This doesn't weaken the finding (the scenario then tests convention-guessing, still not targeting skill) but is an additional internal inconsistency the fix should resolve. (b) The proposed improvement is incomplete: certified-runner.ts:260 embeds the full initialState JSON, and createInitialBattleshipState() includes the opponent's complete ship placements (engine.ts:85-101, no fog of war) — a second answer channel. Even with coordinates stripped from titles, target-priority scenarios remain readable from state, and information-gain/parity scenarios are incoherent under full information; the fix must also redact the hidden fleet from the model-visible state. (c) Scope precision: the 2 base scenarios (battleship.ts:33-70) leak via prompt/state rather than title, so "all 23 generated scenarios" is the right count; severity high is fair for this suite (23/25 scenarios), slightly tempered by the pack's "lightweight" certification tier noted in the prior review.

Two refinements. (1) 'Passing requires only parsing a cell label' is slightly overstated: the title labels use inconsistent coordinate conventions. The engine's canonical label is ROW_LABELS[row]+(column+1) (engine.ts:129-131, matching battleship.ts:117's expected labels), but most titles are transposed (letter=column): 'B1' → {row:0,column:1} = canonical 'A2'; 'B3' → {2,1} = canonical 'C2'. Meanwhile 'parity edge A10' → {0,9} and 'edge scan J1' → {9,0} follow the canonical convention, and diagonal cells (J10, E5, F6, H8, I9) are convention-invariant. So naive title parsing under either single convention fails a subset; the model must guess the convention per scenario or cross-reference ship positions. This doesn't weaken the finding — it makes the benchmark measure convention-guessing rather than targeting. (2) The finding omits a compounding second leak: the prompt includes the full initial state JSON (certified-runner.ts:260), and createInitialBattleshipState (engine.ts:85-127) serializes both boards' complete ship placements including boards.orange.ships[*].cells — the entire enemy fleet layout is in every prompt, which is how ship-anchored title coordinates get disambiguated and which also trivializes the 2 non-generated base scenarios. Any fix should redact enemy ship cells from the serialized state in addition to removing coordinates from titles. Count/anchors confirmed: 23 generated scenarios, line 108 correct, severity 'high' reasonable given it invalidates 23/25 of the pack's discriminative signal on the certified leaderboard (though the pack is lightweight-tier, which a triager could use to argue medium).

findings.summary: 25 scenarios encode ~2 distinct decision archetypes: 24 of 25 use the identical fresh no-shots default board with one generic prompt, so the suite cannot distinguish a smart hunt/target player from a title-parser — a genuinely smart player with the leak withheld would actually FAIL most of it.
findings.evidence: battleship.ts:104-122 all 23 generated scenarios share initialState createInitialBattleshipState() (zero shots) and the same prompt string; battleship.ts:18 `firstShotState = createInitialBattleshipState()` (misnamed — no shot fired), so the base corner scenario is the same archetype. N=25 scenarios vs M=2 decisions: (a) emit a ship cell visible in state/title (18), (b) emit a title-named empty cell (7). On a fresh board there is no board-state reason 'battleship middle at B4' beats B3 or any other ship cell — the exact-match key (validation.ts:387-393) is the only differentiator. A checkerboard/probability-density player without title-parsing scores ~0 correct; a title-parser with no battleship reasoning scores ~100 on group (a).
findings.proposedImprovement: Replace the 23 generated clones with ~10 scenarios that each embed a distinct mid-game shot history in state (built via fireBattleshipShot sequences or createRandomBattleshipBoard with a per-scenario-id seed) so each has a derivable, engine-verifiable best-move set: single-hit orientation probe, two-hit line continuation, blocked-line reversal, sunk-ship-return-to-hunt, endgame size-constraint deduction.
verifierCorrections: Two refinements. (1) The claim "a title-parser with no battleship reasoning scores ~100 on group (a)" is slightly overstated: the generated titles use column-letter/row-number convention ("battleship middle at B4" = {row:3,column:1}), while the base scenarios and the expectedActions labels use row-letter/column-number (label built as String.fromCharCode(65+row) + (column+1), so the same {row:3,column:1} is labeled "D2"). A naive title parser must guess the right convention; picking the base scenarios' convention scores ~0 on off-diagonal generated cells. The core point stands — the expected coordinate is derivable only from the title (possibly disambiguated by cross-referencing ship cells in the state JSON), never from board-state reasoning — and this convention inconsistency is itself an additional defect worth noting in any fix. (2) Group (a)'s 18 includes the follow-line base scenario, which does embed real shot history and is a legitimately derivable two-hit line continuation; so it is 17, not 18, scenarios where the answer is purely title-keyed on a fresh board (the finding's own "24 of 25" sentence is consistent with this).

Minor refinements: (a) "the exact-match key is the only differentiator" is slightly off — the prompt also includes the full state JSON with the opponent's fleet, so group (a) is solvable by state-reading plus title-parsing; the point stands that zero battleship strategy is required. (b) The suite is noisier than stated: title coordinate conventions are inconsistent — ship titles use column-letter+row-number ("B4" -> {row:3,column:1}) while parity-edge "A10" -> {row:0,column:9} and edge-scan "J1" -> {row:9,column:0} only match the engine's row-letter+column-number targetToLabel convention, and the label computed at battleship.ts:117 (row-letter based, e.g. "D2" for the "B4" scenario) contradicts most titles. So even a perfect title-parser can miss up to 2 of the 7 group (b) scenarios; any fix should also unify the labeling convention with engine targetToLabel. (c) "A title-parser scores ~100 on group (a)" understates it — it scores near-100 on group (b) too (5/7 unambiguous). (d) The pack's certificationTier is "lightweight" (packs.ts:34), which slightly tempers severity, but "high" remains defensible since battleship contributes 25 leaderboard scenarios labeled as targeting intelligence.

Minor precision fixes: (1) The title-parser measured 17/18 on group (a) — it misses `follow-line` because that title has no coordinate (the answer sits in the prompt text instead) — but it also passes 5/7 of group (b), so its overall 22/25 / 90.48 is even stronger than the finding's "~100 on group (a)" framing. (2) The smart player scores 2/25 correct (8%, not literally 0), and its aggregate GameIQ score is 36.52 rather than ~0 because legality/structured/latency components floor the score. (3) Additional supporting defect not in the finding: title coordinate conventions are internally inconsistent — ship-cell titles use letter=column ("battleship top at B3" = {row:2,column:1}) while parity-edge "A10" ({row:0,column:9}) and edge-scan "J1" ({row:9,column:0}) use letter=row, and the labels generated by expected() (String.fromCharCode(65+row)) contradict the ship-cell titles (title "B3" → stored label "C2"), so even perfect instruction-following cannot reach 100 without per-scenario convention guessing. (4) runner.ts distinctGroupKey dedup does not mitigate the clones: all 25 scenarios have distinct expectedActions (the two {0,2} scenarios differ in note), so each counts individually.

findings.summary: The 7 'information-gain'/'repeat-avoidance' scenarios designate objectively bad or arbitrary moves as the sole correct answer: corners/edges (J10, A10, J1) are minimum-probability-density cells, all 7 expected targets are empty water while the leaked state shows exact ship locations, and 'repeat-avoidance' scenarios contain zero prior shots to avoid.
findings.evidence: battleship.ts:95-101 — expected targets {9,9},{0,9},{4,4},{5,5},{9,0},{5,4},{7,7} are all non-ship cells on the default board (ships per engine.ts:88-94), yet initialState leaks the ships, so a rational player firing at a known ship cell is scored wrong on all 7. Corner J10 is the worst first shot under any placement-density model (a size-5 carrier covers a corner in only 2 orientations vs 10+ for center cells). Tags claim 'repeat-avoidance' (battleship.ts:95,101) but shotsReceived is empty. validateScenarioCategory (validation.ts:254-267) has no battleship branch, so nothing ever checks these claims; category is hardcoded 'target-priority' for every scenario (battleship.ts:38,109) regardless of tag.
findings.proposedImprovement: Delete or rebuild all 7: with a redacted view, a real information-gain scenario should accept the engine-computed set of maximum-placement-count cells (multi-expectedActions with weight 1 for argmax cells, e.g. the center 4-16 cells on an empty board, partial weight for other correct-parity cells — actionMatchesExpected already takes max over weighted expectedActions, validation.ts:360-366). Real repeat-avoidance needs a dense mid-game shot history where only a few legal cells remain. Also add a battleship branch to validateScenarioCategory that engine-verifies each tag's claim (sink-completion target must sink a ship given shotsReceived; follow-up must be collinear-adjacent to a hit run).
verifierCorrections: Minor precision points: (a) The scenario titles explicitly name the expected cell (e.g. 'choose unrepeated parity corner J10') and the title is included in the prompt (certified-runner.ts:258), so in practice these are coordinate-parroting/instruction-following tests; 'a rational player firing at a known ship cell is scored wrong' requires the model to ignore the title's named coordinate. This softens that one sentence but does not rescue the design — the tags still misrepresent the tested skill, and a model reasoning about the (leaked, fully-known) state is penalized. (b) The repeat-avoidance tag also appears on line 96 (parity-edge), not only 95 and 101. (c) Additional supporting defect: the title coordinate convention is inconsistent with the expectedAction label convention — center-scan's title 'E6' (column-letter/row-number) vs generated label 'F5' (row-letter/column-number, battleship.ts:117) for the same target {row:5,column:4}, so even a compliant model can decode the named cell to a different coordinate.

Two refinements: (a) "a rational player firing at a known ship cell is scored wrong on all 7" slightly overstates 6 of the 7 — the scenario title spells out the target coordinate (e.g. "choose unrepeated parity corner J10"), so a title-parroting model passes those; the real defect there is that strategy-tagged scenarios measure title parsing while punishing genuine Battleship reasoning. (b) The finding understates center-scan: its title says "E6" but the expected target {row:5,column:4} is "F5" under the engine's row-letter labeling (targetToLabel, engine.ts:129-130); "E6" per that convention is {row:4,column:5} — a cruiser cell — so even perfect instruction-following fails that scenario unless the model guesses a transposed column-letter convention. Also minor: any expectedAction weight > 0 marks the scenario "correct" (runner.ts:113, quality > 0), so the proposed partial-weight cells would count as fully correct in outcomeScore, only differing in moveQuality — worth noting when rebuilding the scenarios.

One material nuance the finding omits: the scenario title is included in the model prompt (certified-runner.ts:258) and explicitly names the expected coordinate ("choose unrepeated parity corner J10", "choose central information target E5"), so a literal instruction-follower can still score 1 — the cases are not strictly unanswerable; they degenerate into coordinate-echo tests that penalize strategic play. This slightly softens "arbitrary" but strengthens the mislabeling claim. Additional supporting defect not in the finding: the engine's label convention is letter=row (targetToLabel, engine.ts:129-130), so for the asymmetric targets a model using the common letter=column convention that faithfully follows the title still scores 0 on parity-edge (A10={0,9}), edge-scan (J1={9,0}), and center-scan (E6={4,5}). Minor: line 38's category applies to the hand-authored base scenario (which is genuinely target-priority); the hardcoding issue for the 7 flagged cases is at line 109. Severity "high" is reasonable: 7 of 25 battleship scenarios (28%) are affected.

findings.summary: Coordinate conventions are internally inconsistent: ship-cell titles use column-letter+row-number ('battleship top at B3' for {row:2,column:1}) while the scenario's own expected label, the engine's targetToLabel, and the parity/edge-scan titles use row-letter+column-number — so a perfect candidate applying one consistent convention cannot score 100.
findings.evidence: battleship.ts:83 title 'B3' for {row:2,column:1}, but the generated label at battleship.ts:117 computes 'C2' for the same cell (String.fromCharCode(65+row) + column+1, matching engine targetToLabel, engine.ts:129-131); battleship.ts:96 parity-edge 'A10' for {0,9} follows the engine convention, while battleship.ts:100 center-scan 'E6' for {5,4} (engine label F5) is transposed again. The prompt never defines the convention, and the JSON answer is raw row/column indices — for center-scan a model using standard Battleship notation (row letter) answers {row:4,column:5} and is marked wrong.
findings.proposedImprovement: Pick one convention (the engine's targetToLabel: row letter A-J + column 1-10), regenerate every title/label from targetToLabel(item.target) instead of hand-typed strings, and state the convention explicitly in the prompt ('rows A-J map to row 0-9; columns 1-10 map to column 0-9'). Add an authoring-time assertion that title-embedded labels equal targetToLabel(expected).
verifierCorrections: caseIds 'all-23-generated-scenarios' overstates slightly: 4 of the 23 generated targets are transpose-symmetric and convention-immune (J10 {9,9}, E5 {4,4}, F6 {5,5}, H8 {7,7}), and parity-corner/parity-edge/edge-scan already use the engine convention. About 16 scenarios carry convention-dependent titles. Guaranteed-wrong counts per convention: under the engine/row-letter convention only center-scan is strictly unrecoverable (the 15 ship-cell titles are misleading but recoverable by ignoring the coordinate and using the ship-relative description against the state JSON); under the column-letter convention parity-edge and edge-scan fail. Also worth noting the two hand-written base scenarios are inconsistent too: the carrier note 'A1-E1' (line 47) is column-letter while the expected label 'A3' for {row:0,column:2} (line 65) and the prompt's 'A1 and A2' are row-letter. Severity medium is fair.

Minor precision notes: (a) All 16 ship-cell titles (B1-E1, B3-B6, F5-H5, I7-I9, C9/D9) consistently use the transposed column-letter convention against the default board (engine.ts:85-97), and the base-scenario note at battleship.ts:47 ("carrier occupies A1-E1") is transposed too (though notes are not sent to models), while the base prompt at :60 ("A1 and A2") follows the engine convention — the inconsistency also spans base vs generated scenarios. (b) Some titles cited as engine-convention (E5 {4,4}, F6 {5,5}, J10 {9,9}, H8 {7,7}) are diagonal-symmetric and convention-neutral; the contradiction rests on A10/J1 (row-letter) vs E6 (column-letter), which is sufficient. (c) Ship-cell scenarios are in principle recoverable from the semantic description plus the raw state JSON, so the guaranteed-loss cases are the info-gain ones (center-scan vs parity-edge/edge-scan); severity "medium" remains appropriate.

findings.summary: The 16 ship-cell generated scenarios (plus base corner-carrier-hit, which duplicates them) are one decision — 'name a cell of ship X from the fixed board' — re-skinned 17 times across the cells of five ships; deleting 15 of them would not change the score signal.
findings.evidence: battleship.ts:79-94: carrier-second/third/fourth/end are four consecutive cells of the same ship on the same untouched board with the same prompt; likewise 4 battleship cells, 3 cruiser, 3 submarine, 2 destroyer. battleship.ts:34-51 corner-carrier-hit is the same archetype (fresh board, expected carrier cell {0,0}). runner.ts:46-51 distinctGroupKey groups by expectedActions, and every one has a unique expected cell, so each clone counts as a full independent group in outcomeScore — 68% of the pack's weight is one decision.
findings.proposedImprovement: Keep at most one representative per ship-interaction type (e.g. one 'continue a 2-hit line' and one 'complete the last un-hit cell of a nearly-sunk ship', each with real shot history in state) and delete the other ~14; reinvest the scenario budget in the distinct archetypes listed under the coverage-gap finding.
verifierCorrections: The finding understates the problem in one respect: the prompt sent to the model (certified-runner.ts:252-263, gameIqScenarioPrompt) includes the scenario title — which contains the answer coordinate verbatim (e.g. 'follow the carrier line at B1') — plus the full initialState JSON including the opponent's ships arrays with occupied coordinates. So the 17 scenarios are not just one re-skinned decision, they are one *trivially leaked* decision ('emit a coordinate present in the input'). Minor scoping note: the 68% weight figure holds when the battleship pack is run alone; runGameIqScenarios pools groups across all selected packs, so multi-pack runs dilute (but do not remove) the redundancy. Also, per targetToLabel the letter is the ROW (A1-E1 = row 0, cols 0-4), while the generated titles use letter=column notation ('B1' for {row:0,column:1}) — an internal notation inconsistency adjacent to, but not affecting, this finding.

Minor precision notes only: (a) corner-carrier-hit's prompt text differs slightly from the generated template ("Return the target coordinate for the known highest-value carrier hit") — the "same prompt" claim strictly applies to the 16 generated cases; the base case is same-archetype with different wording, as the finding's evidence already frames it. (b) The redundancy is worse than described: the model prompt (certified-runner.ts:258) includes the scenario title, which contains the expected answer coordinate verbatim in all 16 generated cases, so the tested skill degenerates to coordinate echoing.

findings.summary: A pack labeled 'Battleship Targeting' contains none of the canonical targeting skills: no hidden-information view, no single-hit orientation disambiguation, no blocked-line reversal, no sunk-ship-to-hunt transition, no remaining-ship-size constraint reasoning, and only 1 of 25 scenarios has any shot history at all.
findings.evidence: battleship.ts:24-31 followCarrierLineState is the only state with shots (two hits, same row); every other scenario uses the pristine default board (battleship.ts:18,114). The classic hunt/target loop — hit at E5, probe the up-to-4 adjacent cells; hits C5-C6 then miss at C7, reverse to C4; destroyer sunk, resume parity hunt excluding resolved cells; endgame where only a size-2 ship fits one remaining 2-cell gap — has zero representation, even though the engine already supports building all of these via fireBattleshipShot (engine.ts:442-488) and multi-expectedActions with weights is already supported by actionMatchesExpected (validation.ts:360-366).
findings.proposedImprovement: Add (with a redacted tracking view): (1) hunt-after-single-hit — one hit at E5, accept {D5,F5,E4,E6} all weight 1; (2) line-reversal — hits C5,C6, miss C7, unique answer C4; (3) sunk-transition — destroyer sunk, expect any cell from the engine-computed max-density set, weight 0 for cells adjacent to the sunk wreck if adjacency exclusion is the taught rule; (4) size-constraint endgame — only the carrier remains and misses leave exactly one 5-cell window, unique answer inside it; (5) a mid-game parity scenario with ~20 misses on one color class. Each is engine-derivable, so a deterministic perfect player scores 100 and a random-legal player fails.
verifierCorrections: The caseId cited in the finding, 'gameiq-v0.1-battleship-pack', does not exist; the actual pack id is 'gameiq-v0.1-battleship' (lib/benchmark/gameiq/packs.ts:30). Also, the finding could be strengthened: not only is there no redacted tracking view, the harness sends the complete state JSON including opponent ship placements to the model (lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts:260), so even the two shot-history scenarios test coordinate lookup rather than inference. The proposed improvement's 'redacted tracking view' would require either a redacted initialState variant or a change to gameIqScenarioPrompt serialization, since validation currently reads ship data from initialState.

(a) The proposed "redacted tracking view" is not purely additive scenario data: certified-runner.ts serializes initialState wholesale into the prompt, so hidden-information scenarios require a small harness change (redact ships from the prompt view while validation keeps the full state) plus a GAMEIQ_PROMPT_SET_VERSION bump; the scoring contract itself (actionMatchesExpected, quality in [0,1]) is unaffected. (b) Weight-0 expectedActions are behaviorally identical to omitting the cell (correct = quality > 0), so proposal item 3's weight-0 entries are documentation-only. (c) No engine utility computes a max-density set; that set must be derived offline by the scenario author. (d) The finding understates the problem: generated scenario titles are included in the model prompt and literally contain the expected coordinate, so 23/25 scenarios are answerable without any board reasoning at all.

caseId should be "gameiq-v0.1-battleship" (the pack id in lib/benchmark/gameiq/packs.ts:30); "gameiq-v0.1-battleship-pack" does not exist in the repo. Additionally the finding understates the problem: beyond the full-information state JSON, the scenario title embedded in the prompt leaks the expected coordinate verbatim (e.g. "J10"), so most scenarios are answerable by string extraction alone; any redesign should also stop putting the answer in the title. Severity "high" is appropriate for the pack's validity, with the caveat that the pack is already flagged as certificationTier "lightweight".

findings.summary: Known from prior review (#67): binary single-coordinate exact-match against the one fixed, memorizable createDefaultBoard layout across all 25 scenarios means legality-plus-memorization, not targeting inference, is what is scored.
findings.evidence: Prior review #67 (docs/bench/review-2026-07-01.md:1393-1407) confirmed all 25 scenarios share the deterministic default board and pure exact-match scoring; the current tree's rewritten follow-line base scenario (battleship.ts:53-69, expects the unique continuation {0,2} after hits at A1+A2) addresses the arbitrary-adjacent-cell half, but the fixed-board memorization half still stands.
findings.proposedImprovement: As #67 already suggests: seed createRandomBattleshipBoard(rng) per scenario id so answer keys are derived rather than hand-coded against one static layout.
verifierCorrections: Minor only: the caseId "all-25-battleship-scenarios" is a descriptor, not an actual case id (real ids follow gameiq-v0.1-battleship-<slug>). The finding could be strengthened: the 23 generated scenarios' titles literally contain the expected coordinate (e.g. "target the battleship top at B3"), and the prompt announces "the fixed default board", making the memorization/gameability concern even more direct than the finding states.

Mechanism correction: "memorization" is not required to ace the suite. gameIqScenarioPrompt (lib/benchmark/gameiq/certified-runner.ts:258-262) embeds the full initialState JSON in the prompt, and BattleshipGameState.boards includes BOTH fleets' ship cell coordinates — the opponent layout (i.e., the answer key) is disclosed to the model. The suite therefore measures legality + JSON coordinate lookup, not memorization and not targeting inference; under full disclosure the "information-gain"/"parity"/"repeat-avoidance" scenarios are conceptually meaningless (the expected cell is one arbitrary pick among many equally legal cells). The fixed-board/contamination gameability half still stands (constant lookup table keyed by scenario id). Fix correction: seeding createRandomBattleshipBoard per scenario id alone is insufficient — a fixed seed per id still yields a deterministic, forever-fixed answer key (still lookup-table gameable), and while the full opponent fleet is serialized into the prompt, randomized boards merely change which JSON the model reads the answer from. A real fix must also redact the opponent board from the serialized state (provide only shot history/known hits) and derive expected-action sets (e.g., line-consistent continuations) from the seeded board. Severity: medium is defensible given the misleading "Targeting"/information-gain labeling, though the explicit lightweight certificationTier (lib/benchmark/gameiq/packs.ts:34) and the prior verifier's nit rating for #67 argue it sits at the low end of medium.

findings.summary: latencyFactor is still computed per scenario and averaged into metrics, but the working-tree scoring weights no longer include it, so it is dead output (the prior review's fix for raw-latency scoring, #35, removed the weight but left the plumbing).
findings.evidence: runner.ts:116 and runner.ts:172 compute/average latencyFactor; lib/benchmark/scoring/gameiq.ts:4-9 weights sum to 1.0 with no latency term.
findings.proposedImprovement: Either delete latencyFactor from GameIqScenarioResult/metrics or document it as informational-only, so future readers do not reintroduce it into the score.
verifierCorrections: The "removed the weight but left the plumbing" framing slightly mischaracterizes intent: docs/superpowers/plans/2026-07-01-benchmark-phase-3-low-nit.md (lines 836, 885) shows the fields were deliberately kept "for display/diagnostics" when the 0.05 weight was removed, not accidentally forgotten. However, that intent is documented only in the plan doc, not in code, so the proposed fix (delete or document as informational-only in code) remains apt. Also worth adding to the evidence: lib/benchmark/scoring/types.ts:41 still includes latencyFactor in GameIqScoreInput — an unread field inside the scoring input type — which is the most likely vector for a future reader reintroducing it into the score.

The weight removal is already committed (commit 0d26787, PR #59), not an uncommitted working-tree edit — "working-tree" phrasing is accurate but could imply otherwise. The fix should also remove latencyFactor from GameIqScoreInput in lib/benchmark/scoring/types.ts:41 (and the fixture values at scripts/test-benchmark-scoring.mts:104,113), since that required-field declaration on the scoring input is the most misleading remnant; deleting only the runner-side plumbing at runner.ts:35-39/116/172 and gameiq/types.ts:103 would leave it.
